title: Pointer Distance Calculator
prompt_md:
## Goal
Given a C-style array of integers, use pointer arithmetic to find the element at a given offset and compute the distance between two pointed-to positions.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer `n` (2 <= n <= 20), the number of elements.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers, the array elements.
- Line 3: two space-separated zero-based indices `i` and `j` (0 <= i, j < n).

## Output
- Line 1: the value of the element at index `i`, accessed using pointer arithmetic (not subscript).
- Line 2: the value of the element at index `j`, accessed using pointer arithmetic.
- Line 3: the signed distance `j - i` (as a number of elements) computed by subtracting two pointers.

## Requirements
1. Declare a C-style `int` array of size 20 and read `n` elements into it.
2. Create a pointer `ptr` that points to the first element of the array.
3. Access the elements at positions `i` and `j` using `*(ptr + i)` and `*(ptr + j)` -- do not use the subscript operator `[]` for these accesses.
4. Compute the distance by subtracting the two pointers: `(ptr + j) - (ptr + i)`, and print the result.
5. Use `#include <iostream>` only. Do not use `std::array` or `std::vector`.

test_cases.label: Hidden -- same index gives zero distance

title: Reverse Array via Pointer Iteration
prompt_md:
## Goal
Read an array of integers and print its elements in reverse order using pointer arithmetic to iterate -- no index variables or subscript operators allowed in the reversal logic.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 20), the number of elements.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers.

## Output
- A single line containing the elements in reverse order, separated by spaces, with a trailing newline.

## Requirements
1. Declare a C-style `int` array of size 20 and read `n` elements into it.
2. Create two pointers: `begin` pointing to the first element, and `end` pointing one past the last element (`arr + n`).
3. Iterate backward from `end` to `begin` using pointer decrement. Start your pointer at `end`, decrement it, and dereference to get each element from last to first.
4. Print each element followed by a space (a trailing space on the line is acceptable).
5. Do not use the subscript operator `[]` anywhere in the reversal/printing loop. You may use `[]` only for reading input.
6. Do not use `std::array`, `std::vector`, or any algorithm headers.
